scenarioName: CHF Exacerbation — Hypertensive Flash Pulmonary Edema, CPAP and High-Dose Nitrates

scenarioStory.history: 76-year-old female with HFpEF, longstanding hypertension, and atrial fibrillation woke up at 2 a.m. unable to breathe. She tried to sleep propped up on three pillows but could not get comfortable; over the next 60–90 minutes the dyspnea worsened, and she began coughing up a small amount of pink-tinged sputum. Her husband called 911 when she became unable to walk to the bathroom. On further questioning, she admits she has been skipping her furosemide for the past 3 days because it 'made me get up at night to pee' and she went out to a diner with her sister yesterday and ate 'a lot of salty food.' She denies chest pain, denies fever or cough productive of yellow/green sputum, denies erectile-dysfunction medication use (none in the household), and denies recent angina-like symptoms. Her home BP cuff at her bedside read '210 over something' before EMS arrived. She has had two prior CHF admissions in the past year (no intubations) and no ICU stays.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 76-year-old woman in textbook hypertensive flash pulmonary edema: tripoding on the edge of the bed, audible coarse rales, JVD, pink frothy sputum, severe hypertension, and SpO2 84% on room air. Initial monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 122 with no acute ST changes; she is anxious and can speak only 1–2 words at a time. Importantly, this is NOT bronchospasm — there is no wheezing, no asthma history, no shark-fin capnogram. The protocol pathway for hypertensive pulmonary edema is CPAP + high-dose SL nitroglycerin (0.8 mg q5 min because SBP ≥160), with consideration for an IV nitroglycerin infusion. Diuretics are NOT first-line in the field per protocol — pulmonary edema is a problem of volume distribution, not total body fluid, and inappropriate diuresis can harm. → CPAP applied; 0.8 mg SL nitro given: Crew applies CPAP at 5 cm H2O, then up to 10 cm H2O with FiO2 100%. Patient tolerates the mask after coaching. Sublingual nitroglycerin 0.8 mg given (SBP ≥160 protocol dose) and repeated at 5-minute intervals while SBP remains >100. Crew also prepares IV nitroglycerin infusion per protocol consideration. Within 10 minutes of CPAP + nitrates, BP falls toward the high-normal range, work of breathing decreases, SpO2 rises to 94%, and the patient is now able to speak in short sentences. → Improving on CPAP + nitro; transport: Patient transported supine-of-comfort (Fowler's) on CPAP with continuous monitoring. SL nitro repeated q5 min while SBP remains >100; IV nitroglycerin infusion either started en route or held in reserve depending on response. Continued reassessment of BP, SpO2, EtCO2, and lung exam. Bedside handoff includes initial vitals, CPAP settings, nitro dose timeline, and screening for PDE5 inhibitors documented.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Severe shortness of breath that began about 90 minutes ago and rapidly worsened; she cannot lie flat, is producing pink frothy sputum, and cannot finish a sentence.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 76-year-old woman in textbook hypertensive flash pulmonary edema: tripoding on the edge of the bed, audible coarse rales, JVD, pink frothy sputum, severe hypertension, and SpO2 84% on room air. Initial monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 122 with no acute ST changes; she is anxious and can speak only 1–2 words at a time. Importantly, this is NOT bronchospasm — there is no wheezing, no asthma history, no shark-fin capnogram. The protocol pathway for hypertensive pulmonary edema is CPAP + high-dose SL nitroglycerin (0.8 mg q5 min because SBP ≥160), with consideration for an IV nitroglycerin infusion. Diuretics are NOT first-line in the field per protocol — pulmonary edema is a problem of volume distribution, not total body fluid, and inappropriate diuresis can harm.
relatedChecklist: Identify pulmonary edema (rales, JVD, pink sputum) — NOT bronchospasm; NOT pneumonia
High-flow O2 via NRB initially → escalate to CPAP; target SpO2 94–98%
Cardiac monitor + 12-lead EKG; rule out STEMI/ACS as driver
IV access for medication administration; lock or TKO — DO NOT bolus fluids
Withhold albuterol (no bronchospasm); withhold field furosemide (not first-line)

scenarioEvents.name: CPAP applied; 0.8 mg SL nitro given
scenarioEvents.description: Crew applies CPAP at 5 cm H2O, then up to 10 cm H2O with FiO2 100%. Patient tolerates the mask after coaching. Sublingual nitroglycerin 0.8 mg given (SBP ≥160 protocol dose) and repeated at 5-minute intervals while SBP remains >100. Crew also prepares IV nitroglycerin infusion per protocol consideration. Within 10 minutes of CPAP + nitrates, BP falls toward the high-normal range, work of breathing decreases, SpO2 rises to 94%, and the patient is now able to speak in short sentences.
relatedChecklist: CPAP 5→10 cm H2O FiO2 100%; coach mask seal; reassess WOB and SpO2
Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL q5 min while SBP >100 (SBP ≥160 protocol dose)
Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to BP for sustained afterload reduction
Screen for PDE5 inhibitor use within 48 hr (sildenafil/tadalafil/vardenafil) before nitro
BP, SpO2, EtCO2, lung exam after each nitro dose; document response

scenarioEvents.name: Improving on CPAP + nitro; transport
scenarioEvents.description: Patient transported supine-of-comfort (Fowler's) on CPAP with continuous monitoring. SL nitro repeated q5 min while SBP remains >100; IV nitroglycerin infusion either started en route or held in reserve depending on response. Continued reassessment of BP, SpO2, EtCO2, and lung exam. Bedside handoff includes initial vitals, CPAP settings, nitro dose timeline, and screening for PDE5 inhibitors documented.
relatedChecklist: Continue CPAP through transport; reassess WOB, SpO2, EtCO2 q5 min
Repeat NTG 0.8 mg SL q5 min while SBP >100; or run IV NTG infusion en route
Transport upright; bedside handoff with vitals trend, CPAP settings, NTG timeline

scenarioEvents.name: Albuterol given; CPAP delayed
scenarioEvents.description: Crew misread the coarse, low-pitched rales as wheezing and started a 5 mg albuterol/0.5 mg ipratropium nebulizer treatment without applying CPAP or giving nitroglycerin. The neb is a small mask the patient is barely tolerating, the bronchodilator does nothing for the pulmonary edema, and the underlying problem worsens. SpO2 falls to 80%, BP remains severely elevated at 224/120, and the patient becomes lethargic and slumps forward — impending respiratory failure. The protocol pathway is unambiguous: a CHF/pulmonary-edema pattern (rales, JVD, peripheral edema, CHF history, no wheeze) goes to CPAP + nitrates, NOT albuterol. The crew must now stop the neb, apply CPAP urgently (or BVM with PEEP if CPAP no longer tolerated), and give SL nitroglycerin 0.8 mg.
relatedChecklist: Stop nebulizer; recognize CHF pattern (rales, JVD, edema, no wheeze)
CPAP 10 cm H2O FiO2 100% NOW (or BVM with PEEP if CPAP not tolerated)
Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL NOW; repeat q5 min while SBP >100
Screen PDE5 inhibitor (sildenafil/tadalafil) past 48 hr before any nitro
Document pattern misread; debrief on rales-vs-wheeze + capnogram contour
